title: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections
authors: William R. Bishai, John R. Murphy

items.subtopic: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding the Pathogenic Mechanism of Diphtheria Toxin Production
items.content: Toxigenic strains of C. diphtheriae produce a protein toxin under iron-limiting conditions, leading to systemic toxicity, myocarditis, and polyneuropathy. This pathogenic mechanism is crucial for understanding disease progression.
keyPoints: C. diphtheriae produces a protein toxin causing systemic effects.
Iron-limiting conditions optimize the expression of diphtheria toxin.
Toxigenic conversion can occur in situ via lysogenic conversion by corynebacteriophage beta.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY
items.title: Cultivation and Identification of C. diphtheriae
items.content: The identification and isolation of C. diphtheriae require specific laboratory media that utilize tellurite, colistin, or nalidixic acid for selective growth from pharyngeal samples.
keyPoints: C. diphtheriae is a gram-positive bacillus with a characteristic club-shaped appearance.
Selective isolation uses media containing tellurite, colistin, or nalidixic acid.
The organism can be isolated in both toxigenic and nontoxigenic phenotypes.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY

items.title: Diphtheria Outbreaks and Risk Factors
items.content: Despite effective vaccination programs, diphtheria outbreaks have occurred sporadically in various regions due to lapses in immunization coverage. The disease is more prevalent in areas lacking mass immunization efforts.
keyPoints: Sporadic outbreaks of diphtheria occurred in the 1970s in the US and 1990s in Europe.
Diphtheria remains common in parts of Africa, Asia, Latin America, and the Caribbean due to lack of enforced immunization programs.
Large-scale epidemics happened in post-Soviet states in the late 1990s and Nigeria and Yemen during 2022–2023.
Risk factors for outbreaks include alcoholism, low socioeconomic status, crowded living conditions, and Native American ethnic background.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Transmission and Infectiousness of Diphtheria
items.content: Diphtheria is transmitted via aerosol from close contact with an infected person. Untreated individuals are infectious for approximately 18.5 days, with a basic reproduction number (R0) between 1.7 and 4.3.
keyPoints: C. diphtheriae transmission occurs through the aerosol route during close contact.
Untreated respiratory diphtheria patients are infectious for ~18.5 days.
The basic reproduction number (R0) is 1.7–4.3.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Impact of Vaccination on Diphtheria Incidence
items.content: Vaccination has dramatically reduced diphtheria incidence in Western countries, but immunity wanes over time and pockets of colonization persist. Global vaccination coverage remains a challenge.
keyPoints: Diphtheria antitoxin (1898) and toxoid vaccine (1924) led to near elimination in the West.
U.S. diphtheria cases peaked at 206,000 in 1921; recent rates are very low with only 14 cases from 1996–2018.
Immunity decreases over time; ~30% of men aged 60-69 have subprotective antitoxin titers.
Global vaccination coverage is estimated at ~84%, but reported diphtheria cases were 8638 in 2021.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Mechanism of Diphtheria Toxin Action
items.content: Diphtheria toxin, produced by tox⁺ strains of C. diphtheriae, is a primary virulence factor that inhibits protein synthesis in sensitive species like humans through NAD⁺-dependent ADP-ribosylation of elongation factor 2.
keyPoints: Toxin is a 535-amino-acid single-chain protein with a 50% lethal dose of ~100 ng/kg in humans.
The toxin binds to its cell surface receptor and enters the cytosol via receptor-mediated endocytosis.
Inhibition of protein synthesis by ADP-ribosylation leads to irreversible cell death.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNOLOGY

items.title: Diphtheria Toxoid and Immunity
items.content: Formalinization of diphtheria toxin produces a nontoxic toxoid that elicits antibodies neutralizing the toxin, preventing disease. Protective antitoxin titers (>0.01 U/mL) reduce the risk of diphtheria.
keyPoints: Diphtheria toxoid was developed in 1926 and is used for immunization.
Antibodies from toxoid neutralize the toxin, preventing disease manifestations.
Protective antitoxin titers (>0.01 U/mL) are associated with a low risk of diphtheria.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNOLOGY

items.title: Recognizing Diphtheria in Severe Pharyngitis Cases
items.content: Consider diphtheria when a patient presents with severe pharyngitis, especially if accompanied by difficulty swallowing, respiratory compromise, or systemic signs like myocarditis. The presence of a pseudomembrane is a key diagnostic clue.
keyPoints: Diphtheria should be considered in cases of severe pharyngitis with pseudomembranes.
Common causes of pharyngitis include respiratory viruses (~25%), group A streptococci (15–30%), and atypical bacteria like Mycoplasma pneumoniae (15–20%).
Pseudomembrane presence prompts consideration of diphtheria, distinguishing it from other causes.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Systemic Manifestations and Prognosis in Diphtheria
items.content: Diphtheria toxin leads to systemic effects such as neurotoxicity, causing weakness, and myocarditis, leading to cardiac arrhythmias or heart failure. Large pseudomembranes indicate severe disease with poor prognosis.
keyPoints: Systemic manifestations include neurotoxicity (weakness) and myocarditis (cardiac issues).
Large pseudomembranes are associated with severe disease and poor prognosis.
Bull-neck diphtheria, characterized by massive tonsillar swelling, indicates advanced disease.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Diagnosis and Differentiation of Diphtheria
items.content: Diagnose diphtheria based on clinical presentation (sore throat, pseudomembranes) and laboratory confirmation (isolation of C. diphtheriae). Differentiate from streptococcal pharyngitis by the tightly adherent nature of the pseudomembrane.
keyPoints: Diagnosis requires isolation of C. diphtheriae or compatible organisms.
CDC defines confirmed and probable respiratory diphtheria based on laboratory and clinical criteria.
Pseudomembranes in diphtheria are tightly adherent, unlike those in streptococcal pharyngitis.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Airway Obstruction and Cardiomyopathy as Early Diphtheria Complications
items.content: In advanced diphtheria, airway obstruction is a critical early complication, contributing to 60–65% of deaths within the first 1–2 weeks. Pseudomembranes can obstruct the airway or progress to the larynx and tracheobronchial tree, with children being particularly vulnerable due to smaller airways. Late toxic manifestations include cardiomyopathy, which accounts for 20–25% of deaths and is associated with arrhythmias and dilated cardiomyopathy.
keyPoints: Airway obstruction: 60-65% of early deaths (1-2 weeks post-symptoms).
Children are more prone to airway obstruction due to smaller airways.
Cardiomyopathy: Accounts for 20-25% of deaths, associated with arrhythmias and dilated cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · COMPLICATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Polyneuropathy in Diphtheria: Late-Onset Neurological Complications
items.content: Polyneuropathy typically develops 3–5 weeks after diphtheria onset, potentially accounting for 15% of deaths. It manifests with sensory and motor deficits starting in the cranial nerves, progressing to respiratory and abdominal muscle weakness, and extremity paresis. Despite its severity, polyneuropathy is often reversible if the patient survives the acute phase.
keyPoints: Polyneuropathy onset: 3-5 weeks post-diphtheria.
Accounts for 15% of deaths.
Manifestations include cranial nerve dysfunction, respiratory and abdominal muscle weakness, extremity paresis.
Typically reversible if the patient survives the acute phase.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · COMPLICATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Why Prompt Administration of Diphtheria Antitoxin is Crucial
items.content: The timely administration of diphtheria antitoxin is essential in managing respiratory diphtheria to reduce the extent of local disease and prevent complications such as myocarditis and neuropathy. Since the antitoxin cannot neutralize cell-bound toxin, early initiation significantly lowers mortality risk.
keyPoints: Diphtheria antitoxin reduces local disease severity and complication risks (myocarditis, neuropathy).
Early administration is associated with reduced mortality risk.
Antitoxin does not neutralize cell-bound toxin; hence, prompt initiation is critical.
Obtained from CDC Emergency Operations Center after contacting state health department.
Test dose required to rule out immediate hypersensitivity; desensitization needed if hypersensitive.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · DIPHTHERIA ANTITOXIN | ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.title: Antimicrobial Therapy in Diphtheria Management
items.content: Antibiotics are essential for preventing transmission, halting toxin production, and reducing infection severity. Erythromycin or penicillin G is recommended until the patient can swallow comfortably, followed by oral continuation to complete a 14-day course.
keyPoints: Antibiotics prevent transmission and further toxin production.
Erythromycin: 500 mg IV q6h for adults; 40–50 mg/kg per day in children until swallowing is possible, then 500 mg PO qid for 14 days.
Procaine penicillin G: 600,000 U IM q12h for adults; 12,500–25,000 U/kg for children until swallowing is possible, then oral penicillin V 125–250 mg qid for 14 days.
Penicillin may lead to rapid fever resolution and lower resistance rates compared to erythromycin but has higher relapse rates.
Erythromycin stops toxin synthesis faster than cell wall-active agents like penicillin.
Alternative antibiotics include rifampin, clindamycin, clarithromycin, azithromycin, linezolid, and vancomycin.
Repeat throat culture 2 weeks post-therapy to confirm eradication; additional treatment if not eradicated after initial course.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · DIPHTHERIA ANTITOXIN | ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.title: Hospitalization and Monitoring for Suspected Diphtheria
items.content: Patients suspected of having diphtheria should be hospitalized in respiratory isolation rooms with close monitoring of cardiac and respiratory function. A cardiac workup is recommended to assess the possibility of myocarditis, especially given the risk of cardiac complications.
keyPoints: Hospitalization in respiratory isolation rooms
Close monitoring of cardiac and respiratory function
Cardiac workup for assessing myocarditis
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | PROGNOSIS | PREVENTION

items.title: Prognosis Factors in Diphtheria
items.content: The case fatality ratio among unvaccinated, untreated individuals is 29%, with children under age 5 at a 1.5-fold higher risk of mortality. Risk factors for death include bullneck diphtheria, myocarditis with ventricular tachycardia, complete heart block, and the interval between onset and antitoxin administration.
keyPoints: Case fatality ratio: 29% among unvaccinated, untreated
Children under age 5 have a 1.5-fold higher risk of mortality
Risk factors for death include bullneck diphtheria, myocarditis with ventricular tachycardia, complete heart block
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | PROGNOSIS | PREVENTION

items.title: Diphtheria Prevention through Vaccination
items.content: Vaccination is key in preventing diphtheria. The diphtheria toxoid-based vaccine has an efficacy of 87% in preventing symptomatic disease. DTaP and Tdap vaccines are recommended for children and adults, respectively, with specific schedules outlined to maintain immunity.
keyPoints: Diphtheria toxoid-based vaccine efficacy: 87%
DTaP recommended for children up to age 6
Tdap recommended for adolescents and adults as a booster
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | PROGNOSIS | PREVENTION

items.subtopic: OTHER CORYNEBACTERIAL AND RHODOCOCCUS INFECTIONS | MICROBIOLOGY AND LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Recognize the Pathogenic Potential of Nondiphtherial Corynebacteria in Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: While often considered mere colonizers or contaminants, nondiphtherial corynebacteria can cause severe invasive diseases, especially in immunocompromised individuals. These organisms, including C. ulcerans and C. pseudotuberculosis, may produce diphtheria toxin leading to serious illness. They are associated with bloodstream infections linked to catheter use, endocarditis, prosthetic valve infection, meningitis, brain abscesses, osteomyelitis, and peritonitis.
keyPoints: Nondiphtherial corynebacteria can cause invasive disease in immunocompromised patients.
C. ulcerans and C. pseudotuberculosis may produce diphtheria toxin.
Associated with bloodstream infections, particularly related to catheter use.
Risk factors include indwelling intravenous or peritoneal catheters and neurosurgical shunts.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · OTHER CORYNEBACTERIAL AND RHODOCOCCUS INFECTIONS | MICROBIOLOGY AND LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: OTHER CORYNEBACTERIAL AND RHODOCOCCUS INFECTIONS | MICROBIOLOGY AND LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Understand the Epidemiology and Reservoirs of Nondiphtherial Corynebacteria
items.content: Humans are natural reservoirs for several nondiphtherial coryneforms, such as C. xerosis and C. jeikeium. Animal sources like milk can carry C. ulcerans and C. pseudotuberculosis, while soil is a reservoir for R. equi, which causes necrotizing pneumonia in immunocompromised individuals.
keyPoints: Humans are natural reservoirs for several nondiphtherial coryneforms.
Animal sources like milk carry C. ulcerans and C. pseudotuberculosis.
Soil is a reservoir for R. equi, associated with necrotizing pneumonia.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · OTHER CORYNEBACTERIAL AND RHODOCOCCUS INFECTIONS | MICROBIOLOGY AND LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: C. ulcerans Infection: Zoonotic Transmission and Treatment
items.content: C. ulcerans causes a diphtheria-like illness, primarily presenting as exudative pharyngitis in individuals exposed to animals. It is considered a zoonosis, with transmission linked to contact with animal carriers such as horses, cattle, dogs, and pigs.
keyPoints: Infection occurs during summer months, predominantly in rural areas.
Treatment involves antitoxin and antibiotics; erythromycin is first-line.
Contact investigation includes throat cultures for antimicrobial prophylaxis and vaccination with diphtheria toxoid-containing vaccine for unimmunized contacts.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: C. pseudotuberculosis: Human Infection from Animal Contact
items.content: C. pseudotuberculosis, primarily an animal pathogen affecting sheep, can cause human disease such as suppurative granulomatous lymphadenitis and eosinophilic pneumonia syndrome in individuals handling animals.
keyPoints: Surgical excision of affected lymph nodes is recommended when feasible.
Treatment with erythromycin or tetracycline has been successful.
Some strains produce diphtheria toxin, necessitating antitoxin treatment.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: C. jeikeium: Opportunistic Pathogen in Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: C. jeikeium is an opportunistic pathogen associated with sepsis and other severe infections, particularly in neutropenic and HIV-infected patients.
keyPoints: Risk factors include hematologic malignancy, prolonged hospitalization, and exposure to multiple antibiotics.
Resistant to most antibiotics except oxazolidinones (e.g., linezolid) and glycopeptides (e.g., vancomycin).
Infection control protocols are crucial for high-risk patients in intensive care units.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Corynebacterial Infections: Key Pathogens and Antibiotic Susceptibilities
items.content: Nondiphtherial corynebacteria such as C. xerosis, C. amylocatum, and C. striatum can cause invasive infections in immunocompromised individuals or postoperative patients. C. glucuronolyticum causes genitourinary tract infections resistant to fluoroquinolones, macrolides, and tetracyclines but susceptible to $\beta$-lactams, rifampin, aminoglycosides, and vancomycin. R. equi, an intracellular pathogen causing granulomatous necrosis, is treated with antibiotics like macrolides, clindamycin, rifampin, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.
keyPoints: C. xerosis: Invasive infections in immunocompromised or postoperative patients
C. glucuronolyticum: Resistant to fluoroquinolones, macrolides, tetracyclines; susceptible to $\beta$-lactams, rifampin, aminoglycosides, vancomycin
R. equi: Causes granulomatous necrosis, treated with intracellular-penetrating antibiotics like macrolides, clindamycin, rifampin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Rhodococcus equi Infections: Clinical Presentation and Treatment
items.content: R. equi is an intracellular pathogen causing granulomatous necrosis, primarily manifesting as nodular or cavitary pneumonia in the upper lobe, similar to tuberculosis. It affects immunocompromised patients, including those with HIV. Subcutaneous nodules may also occur. Treatment involves antibiotics that penetrate macrophages, such as macrolides, clindamycin, rifampin, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.
keyPoints: R. equi: Causes granulomatous necrosis, primarily upper lobe pneumonia
Affects immunocompromised patients, including those with HIV
Treatment: Macrolides, clindamycin, rifampin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Arcanobacterium haemolyticum Infections: Clinical Features and Antibiotic Resistance
items.content: A. haemolyticum causes pharyngitis with a scarlatiniform rash, often confused with toxic shock syndrome, primarily affecting teenagers. It can also cause bacteremia, soft tissue infections, osteomyelitis, and cavitary pneumonia, especially in diabetic patients. The organism is susceptible to most $\beta$-lactams, macrolides, fluoroquinolones, clindamycin, vancomycin, and doxycycline but shows resistance to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and tetracycline.
keyPoints: A. haemolyticum: Causes pharyngitis with scarlatiniform rash
Associated with bacteremia, soft tissue infections, osteomyelitis, cavitary pneumonia in diabetics
Susceptible to most $\beta$-lactams, macrolides, fluoroquinolones, clindamycin, vancomycin, doxycycline; resistant to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, tetracycline
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.subtopic: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY
items.question: Which characteristic appearance is associated with C. diphtheriae when viewed microscopically?
options: Chinese characters due to clusters of parallel rays or palisades
Spiral-shaped bacilli forming a helical pattern
Cocci arranged in chains resembling strings of pearls
Rod-shaped bacteria with flagella visible at the poles
items.explanation: C. diphtheriae typically forms clusters of parallel rays, or palisades, that are referred to as 'Chinese characters'.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY
items.question: What is the role of corynebacteriophage beta in C. diphtheriae?
options: It carries the structural gene tox, which encodes diphtheria toxin.
It provides resistance to antibiotics used against C. diphtheriae.
It causes the bacteria to become nonmotile and unencapsulated.
It is responsible for the sporulation of C. diphtheriae.
items.explanation: Corynebacteriophage beta carries the structural gene tox, which encodes diphtheria toxin.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY
items.question: Under what conditions is the optimal expression of diphtheria toxin observed in C. diphtheriae?
items.explanation: Growth under iron-limiting conditions leads to the optimal expression of diphtheria toxin.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which region has seen a recent increase in diphtheria cases due to factors such as vaccine hesitancy and migration?
items.explanation: Multiple European nations have reported high rates of diphtheria among unvaccinated asylum seekers.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is the basic reproduction number ($ R_{0} $) for respiratory diphtheria?
items.explanation: The $ R_{0} $ for respiratory diphtheria is 1.7–4.3.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: In which decade did the United States experience its peak annual incidence rate of diphtheria?
items.explanation: The annual incidence rate in the United States peaked in 1921.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which type of diphtheria is more common in tropical latitudes?
items.explanation: In tropical latitudes, cutaneous diphtheria is more common than respiratory diphtheria.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which diphtheria toxin causes cell death?
options: The toxin inhibits protein synthesis by ADP-ribosylation of elongation factor 2.
The toxin disrupts cellular membranes leading to lysis.
The toxin induces apoptosis through caspase activation.
The toxin interferes with DNA replication.
items.explanation: Diphtheria toxin causes cell death by irreversibly inhibiting protein synthesis via ADP-ribosylation of elongation factor 2.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNOLOGY

items.question: What is the primary virulence factor in diphtheria?
items.explanation: The primary virulence factor in diphtheria is the diphtheria toxin produced by tox⁺ strains of C. diphtheriae.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNOLOGY

items.question: What happens to individuals with a diphtheria antitoxin titer of >0.01 U/mL?
options: They are at high risk of contracting diphtheria.
They have no immunity against diphtheria.
They are at low risk of disease.
items.explanation: Individuals with a diphtheria antitoxin titer of >0.01 U/mL are considered to be at low risk of developing the disease.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of diphtheric pseudomembrane?
options: It is loosely attached to underlying tissues.
It is associated with high-grade fever.
items.explanation: The diphtheric pseudomembrane is tightly adherent to the underlying tissues, and attempts to dislodge it may cause bleeding.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which of the following symptoms might be an initial manifestation of diphtheria?
items.explanation: Occasionally, weakness, dysphagia, headache, and voice change are the initial manifestations of diphtheria.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What is a poor prognostic sign in advanced cases of respiratory diphtheria?
items.explanation: In more advanced cases, neck edema and difficulty breathing are evident and carry a poor prognosis.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which systemic manifestation of diphtheria is caused by the effects of diphtheria toxin?
items.explanation: The systemic manifestations of diphtheria from the effects of diphtheria toxin include cardiac arrhythmias or congestive heart failure due to myocarditis.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which complication of diphtheria typically occurs 3–5 weeks after onset and may lead to weakness of respiratory muscles?
items.explanation: Polyneuropathy occurs 3–5 weeks after the onset of diphtheria and can lead to weakness of respiratory muscles.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · COMPLICATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is a common cause of death in the first 1–2 weeks following symptom onset in patients with advanced diphtheria?
items.explanation: Airway obstruction accounts for 60–65% of deaths typically in the first 1–2 weeks after symptom onset.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · COMPLICATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which diagnostic method is NOT used to confirm diphtheria?
options: Cultivation of C. diphtheriae from the site of infection
Demonstration of local lesions with characteristic histopathology
Serum antibody testing for diphtheria toxin
items.explanation: Diphtheria diagnosis is based on clinical signs, symptoms, and laboratory confirmation by cultivation or histopathology, not serum antibody testing.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · COMPLICATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the primary reason for the prompt administration of diphtheria antitoxin in respiratory diphtheria?
options: It reduces the extent of local disease and risk of myocarditis and neuropathy complications.
It prevents transmission to susceptible contacts.
It is used as a substitute for antibiotic therapy.
items.explanation: Diphtheria antitoxin reduces the extent of local disease and risk of complications such as myocarditis and neuropathy.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · DIPHTHERIA ANTITOXIN | ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.question: Why is a test dose of diphtheria antitoxin administered before the full therapeutic dose?
options: To determine the patient's immune response to the toxin.
To rule out immediate hypersensitivity and prevent allergic reactions.
To assess the severity of the infection.
To ensure that the antitoxin is effective against cell-bound toxin.
items.explanation: A test dose is given to rule out immediate hypersensitivity before administering a full therapeutic dose.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · DIPHTHERIA ANTITOXIN | ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.question: Which of the following statements about diphtheria antitoxin is true?
options: It is commercially available in the United States.
It should be administered after antibiotic therapy.
items.explanation: Prompt initiation of diphtheria antitoxin is associated with a significant reduction in mortality risk.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · DIPHTHERIA ANTITOXIN | ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.question: Which of the following is NOT recommended as a management strategy for patients with suspected diphtheria?
options: Close monitoring of cardiac and respiratory function
Treatment with glucocorticoids to reduce myocarditis risk
Consultation with anesthesiology or ENT specialists
items.explanation: Treatment with glucocorticoids has not been shown to reduce the risk of myocarditis.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | PROGNOSIS | PREVENTION

items.question: What is a significant predictor of fatal outcome in diphtheria?
options: The size of the pseudomembrane at initial diagnosis
Interval between onset of local disease and administration of antitoxin
Age under 6 months or over 60 years
items.explanation: The interval between the onset of local disease and the administration of antitoxin is an important predictor.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | PROGNOSIS | PREVENTION

items.question: Which vaccine combination was approved by the U.S. FDA in 2018 for use in children under 5 years old?
items.explanation: The hexavalent vaccine DTaP-IPV-Hib-HepB was approved for use in children under 5 years old.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | PROGNOSIS | PREVENTION

items.question: What is the recommended prophylaxis for close contacts of diphtheria patients?
options: Throat culture followed by oral erythromycin or IM benzathine penicillin G
items.explanation: Prophylaxis includes throat culture followed by oral erythromycin or IM benzathine penicillin G.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | PROGNOSIS | PREVENTION

items.question: Which of the following corynebacteria can produce diphtheria toxin and cause severe human illness?
items.explanation: C. ulcerans may produce diphtheria toxin and cause severe human illness.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · OTHER CORYNEBACTERIAL AND RHODOCOCCUS INFECTIONS | MICROBIOLOGY AND LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is a risk factor for invasive disease caused by nondiphtherial corynebacteria?
items.explanation: Risk factors include indwelling intravenous or peritoneal catheters.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · OTHER CORYNEBACTERIAL AND RHODOCOCCUS INFECTIONS | MICROBIOLOGY AND LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: OTHER CORYNEBACTERIAL AND RHODOCOCCUS INFECTIONS | MICROBIOLOGY AND LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: Which organism is associated with necrotizing pneumonia and granulomatous infection, particularly in immunocompromised individuals?
items.explanation: Rhodococcus equi is associated with necrotizing pneumonia and granulomatous infection.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · OTHER CORYNEBACTERIAL AND RHODOCOCCUS INFECTIONS | MICROBIOLOGY AND LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which organism is considered a zoonosis and can be traced to contact with animal carriers such as dogs and pigs?
items.explanation: C. ulcerans is considered a zoonosis and can be traced to contact with animal carriers.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What type of pneumonia syndrome is associated with C. pseudotuberculosis infection among individuals who handle sheep?
items.explanation: C. pseudotuberculosis causes an eosinophilic pneumonia syndrome.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which organism is associated with a severe urinary tract syndrome known as alkaline-encrusted cystitis?
items.explanation: C. urealyticum is associated with alkaline-encrusted cystitis.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which antibiotic class is effective against C. jeikeium, which is resistant to most other antibiotics?
items.explanation: Oxazolidinones (e.g., linezolid) are effective against C. jeikeium.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which organism has been associated with genitourinary tract infections such as prostatitis and urethritis?
items.explanation: C. glucuronolyticum is associated with male genitourinary tract infections.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which antibiotic class is C. glucuronolyticum resistant to?
items.explanation: C. glucuronolyticum is resistant to fluoroquinolones.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which organism has been identified in children with otitis media and is susceptible to vancomycin?
items.explanation: C. auris has been identified in children with otitis media and is susceptible to vancomycin.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which organism causes a scarlatiniform rash on the trunk and proximal extremities during pharyngitis?
items.explanation: Arcanobacterium haemolyticum pharyngitis is associated with a scarlatiniform rash.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.subtopic: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY
items.statement: C. diphtheriae is a gram-negative bacillus.
items.explanation: C. diphtheriae is described as a gram-positive bacillus.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY
items.statement: Toxigenic strains of C. diphtheriae can cause myocarditis.
items.explanation: The toxin produced by toxigenic strains causes systemic toxicity, including myocarditis.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Diphtheria has been completely eradicated in the United States.
items.explanation: While very low, diphtheria cases still occur, such as a case in 2003 from a returning traveler.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: The mean incubation period for respiratory diphtheria is approximately 1.4 days.
items.explanation: The passage states the mean incubation period is 1.4 days.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Diphtheria toxin is produced in all strains of C. diphtheriae.
items.explanation: Diphtheria toxin is only produced by tox⁺ strains of C. diphtheriae.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNOLOGY

items.statement: Immunization with diphtheria toxoid can prevent most manifestations of the disease.
items.explanation: Diphtheria toxoid immunization elicits antibodies that neutralize the toxin, preventing most disease manifestations.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · PATHOGENESIS AND IMMUNOLOGY

items.statement: The presence of a pharyngeal pseudomembrane should prompt consideration of diphtheria.
items.explanation: A pharyngeal pseudomembrane is an important clinical feature that prompts consideration of diphtheria.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Cutaneous diphtheria typically presents with exudative lesions.
items.explanation: Cutaneous diphtheria is characterized by punched-out ulcerative lesions with necrotic sloughing or pseudomembrane formation, not exudative lesions.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: C. pseudodiphtheriticum poses a significant risk in diphtheria diagnosis.
items.explanation: Corynebacterium pseudodiphtheriticum is nontoxigenic and does not pose a significant risk.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · COMPLICATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Isolation of toxigenic C. diphtheriae is reportable to the state health department in the United States.
items.explanation: All laboratory isolates of toxigenic C. diphtheriae should be reported to the state health department.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · COMPLICATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Erythromycin therapy targets protein synthesis, potentially stopping toxin synthesis more quickly than a cell wall-active β-lactam agent.
items.explanation: Erythromycin targets protein synthesis, which may stop toxin production faster than β-lactam agents.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · DIPHTHERIA ANTITOXIN | ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.statement: Patients with respiratory diphtheria should receive antitoxin levels measured immediately after recovery.
items.explanation: Antitoxin levels should be measured 6 months later if antitoxin has been administered.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · DIPHTHERIA ANTITOXIN | ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.statement: Diphtheria toxoid-based vaccine efficacy is estimated to be over 90% in the prevention of symptomatic disease.
items.explanation: The vaccine efficacy is estimated to be 87%, not over 90%.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | PROGNOSIS | PREVENTION

items.statement: Cutaneous diphtheria has a high mortality rate and is commonly associated with myocarditis.
items.explanation: Cutaneous diphtheria has a low mortality rate and is rarely associated with myocarditis.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | PROGNOSIS | PREVENTION

items.subtopic: OTHER CORYNEBACTERIAL AND RHODOCOCCUS INFECTIONS | MICROBIOLOGY AND LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: Corynebacterium jeikeium and Corynebacterium urealyticum are associated with resistance to multiple antibiotics.
items.explanation: These organisms are indeed associated with resistance to multiple antibiotics.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · OTHER CORYNEBACTERIAL AND RHODOCOCCUS INFECTIONS | MICROBIOLOGY AND LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: OTHER CORYNEBACTERIAL AND RHODOCOCCUS INFECTIONS | MICROBIOLOGY AND LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: Soil is the natural reservoir for C. ulcerans.
items.explanation: C. ulcerans and C. pseudotuberculosis are associated with animal reservoirs, not soil.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · OTHER CORYNEBACTERIAL AND RHODOCOCCUS INFECTIONS | MICROBIOLOGY AND LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: C. ulcerans can cause cutaneous disease.
items.explanation: Cutaneous disease due to C. ulcerans has been reported.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Erythrasma is treated with oral macrolide therapy as a first-line treatment.
items.explanation: Milder cases of erythrasma respond to topical treatments, while more severe infections may require oral macrolides.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: C. imitans has been identified as a cause of pneumonia.
items.explanation: C. imitans is identified as a nontoxigenic cause of pharyngitis, not pneumonia.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: R. equi infections are commonly treated with $\beta$-lactam antibiotics.
items.explanation: $\beta$-Lactam antibiotics have not been useful for treating R. equi infections.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.subtopic: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY
items.assertion: Corynebacteriophage beta is responsible for the toxigenic conversion of C. diphtheriae.
items.reason: It carries the structural gene tox, which encodes diphtheria toxin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections | DIPHTHERIA | ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: Diphtheria outbreaks are more likely in areas with low vaccination coverage.
items.reason: Vaccine hesitancy and socioeconomic instability contribute to lower vaccination rates.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why diphtheria outbreaks occur.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Cutaneous diphtheria is primarily caused by toxigenic strains of C. diphtheriae.
items.reason: Most isolates from cutaneous diphtheria lack the tox gene and do not express diphtheria toxin.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; most cutaneous diphtheria cases are nontoxigenic.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Cutaneous diphtheria is characterized by punched-out ulcerative lesions.
items.reason: The diagnosis requires cultivation of C. diphtheriae from lesions, which most commonly occur on the lower and upper extremities, head, and trunk.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why cutaneous diphtheria is characterized by punched-out ulcerative lesions.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.assertion: Most patients with respiratory diphtheria seek medical care for sore throat and fever several days into the illness.
items.reason: The presence of a pharyngeal pseudomembrane is an early sign that prompts immediate medical attention.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because the pseudomembrane may not be present immediately and patients often seek care several days into the illness.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · Diphtheria | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.assertion: Patients who recover from respiratory or cutaneous diphtheria should receive the appropriate vaccine.
items.reason: To ensure the development of protective antibody titers.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why vaccination is necessary post-recovery to develop protective antibodies.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · DIPHTHERIA ANTITOXIN | ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY

items.assertion: The pseudomembrane in diphtheria may increase in size over time.
items.reason: Fatal pseudomembranous diphtheria typically occurs in patients with nonprotective antibody titers and unimmunized patients.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why the pseudomembrane may increase in size.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | PROGNOSIS | PREVENTION

items.assertion: Tdap is recommended for all adults regardless of their previous vaccination status.
items.reason: Tdap is a tetanus toxoid, reduced diphtheria toxoid, and acellular pertussis vaccine formulated for adolescents and adults.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why Tdap is recommended for all adults.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · MANAGEMENT STRATEGIES | PROGNOSIS | PREVENTION

items.subtopic: OTHER CORYNEBACTERIAL AND RHODOCOCCUS INFECTIONS | MICROBIOLOGY AND LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.assertion: Nondiphtherial corynebacteria are considered colonizers or contaminants.
items.reason: They have been associated with invasive disease, particularly in immunocompromised patients.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why they are considered colonizers.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · OTHER CORYNEBACTERIAL AND RHODOCOCCUS INFECTIONS | MICROBIOLOGY AND LABORATORY DIAGNOSIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: C. jeikeium is an important opportunistic pathogen among neutropenic and HIV-infected patients.
items.reason: C. jeikeium emerged as a significant pathogen after a survey of diseases caused by nondiphtherial corynebacteria in American hospitals.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why C. jeikeium is an important opportunistic pathogen.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: C. urealyticum causes alkaline-encrusted cystitis due to its ability to ferment most carbohydrates.
items.reason: C. urealyticum is urease-positive and associated with severe urinary tract syndrome.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because C. urealyticum does not ferment most carbohydrates; the reason is true.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: C. auris is resistant to penicillin G.
items.reason: C. auris has been identified in children with otitis media and shows resistance to certain antibiotics.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: R. equi is a cause of tuberculosis-like infections.
items.reason: R. equi causes granulomatous necrosis and caseation similar to Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Diphtheria and Other Corynebacterial Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)
